title: First Days
html:
The first bell rang before the light came. He was already awake.

The dining table held four places when he came down. The merchant from the south room was there, eating quickly and reading something on a folded paper. A seasonal worker at the far end was younger than he looked from across the room, eating faster than the merchant, gone out the door with a piece of bread still in his hand before Alden had finished his bowl. The landlady, whose name was Britt, set porridge in front of him and went back to the kitchen without waiting.

He ate and consulted the map against the edge of the table. The market district was three lanes north. The mill was between him and it. He folded the map and washed his bowl at the basin Britt had left by the door and went out.

***

The market coordinator was a stout man in his mid-forties, moving between stalls with a ledger in one hand and a short stick he used to point at things he wanted moved. He stopped when Alden came up.

"Work," Alden said.

The man looked at him. 14-year-old body, cloth bag, alone, walking without the hesitation of someone new to the settlement. "Market day is tomorrow. Today is prep."

"I'm available for prep."

"What can you do?"

"Run messages. Carry parcels. Read a map and use it." He paused. "I know the layout from the eastern lane to the mill. I'll have the rest mapped by midday."

The coordinator studied him for three seconds. Two copper per day, message running and light deliveries, starting now.

He said yes.

He spent the first morning moving through Aldenvale at the coordinator's direction, delivering handwritten notes between merchants and carrying small packages from the storage row to the stalls. He mapped as he moved. By the second hour he had the lane structure well enough to route himself without retracing and was consistently faster than the coordinator's own estimates for delivery time. The coordinator noticed on the third run and said nothing. He gave Alden the longer routes after that.

At the corner of the south lane and the market cross, the Compact collectors were at a dried goods stall. Two of them, the same coat cut as the day before. One was talking to the merchant. The merchant's posture was closed, arms across his chest, weight shifted back. The collector held the ledger steady and pointed at a figure without raising his voice.

The merchant produced a small pouch. The collector made an entry. They separated without further exchange.

Alden completed his delivery to the adjacent stall and moved on. The collection process had been documented, unhurried, clearly expected by the merchant even if unwelcome. A pre-logged debt, satisfied on schedule. The Compact's operation was administrative rather than coercive at this stage, at least for routine collections.

One of the collectors glanced at him as he passed. A 14-year-old with a parcel bag. The glance lasted half a second.

'Registered as negligible. Maintain that.'

He completed the morning's deliveries and reported back to the coordinator at midday. Two copper were placed in his hand.

"Tomorrow's market day," the coordinator said. "Earlier start. Half-bell before dawn."

"I'll be there."

He bought bread at the market price, 1 copper for a day's worth, and ate at the edge of the square watching the stall activity. He used the time to map the social layout: which merchants worked together, which ignored each other, which ones the coordinator treated as priorities. Useful for routing. He finished the bread and went back to work.

***

His routine settled over the next several days. First bell, breakfast, market district by the half-bell, deliveries through the morning, bread at midday, a second round of errands through the afternoon. On market days the pace doubled and the coordinator paid an extra copper. On off-days the work was lighter but consistent.

He spent each evening with the lamp down, sitting in the darkness for one hour before sleep. He counted the units: 1 per hour, 1 per night. He was not certain his early sessions at the cabin had been sufficiently deliberate to count, so he held those tentatively and built from what he was certain of.

On the fifth morning, Britt mentioned at breakfast that old Deric had passed.

"Been ill since the harvest," she said. She was talking to the merchant, not to him. "Family expected it. Still." She named the house in the next sentence, giving directions to the merchant, who wanted to send something from the market as a courtesy.

The house was one lane over from the market district. Alden routed his midday delivery through that lane.

The door was open. A draft moved the window cloth inside. From the lane he could see the body laid out on the main table, cloth drawn to the chin, two candles burning at the head. A woman was standing at the far wall. A man at the near end was speaking to someone Alden could not see from this angle. Neighbors came and went through the door in small clusters.

He stood for the time it took to deliver to the adjacent house. Ninety seconds. He did not go closer. He did not need to.

+8 units.

Running total: 14. Then he counted properly. 5 from before, plus 1 per deliberate dark session for 5 nights: that was 10 units already. Adding the witnessed death brought him to 18.

He recalculated on the walk back to the coordinator. The cabin sessions he'd held tentatively: 1 offering kill at 4 units and 1 dark hour at 1 unit. If those counted, his total was actually 23. If they didn't, 18. He would operate on the conservative number and treat 18 as confirmed.

Twelve units to tier 1, confirmed minimum.

The next afternoon the funeral was forming when he was passing on a delivery. The body had been wrapped for transport. Four adults and two children, a priest from the settlement shrine standing with the nearest man. A fifth adult coming down the lane from the east, who embraced one of the women.

He stopped at the lane's end and stood for 11 minutes before his next delivery required him to move.

+2 units. Total, confirmed: 20. Possibly 25 if the cabin sessions counted.

He worked the conservative number. Ten units remaining, confirmed.

***

On the eighth day he extended the boarding arrangement with Britt to open duration, 3 copper per day. She accepted without asking how long. His coin balance had reached 16 copper over 8 days minus bread and lodging, and was stable enough to plan around. He put 2 copper per day into a separate fold in the jacket against the monthly offering cost and spent the rest on food and lodging without further calculation.

He spent the evenings with the Guide. The Detection and Visibility entry had one line he had not fully processed: at tier 1 and above, the practitioner became detectable to death-domain-aligned entities at close proximity. The Guide listed four entity types. He read each entry.

Two were described as passive: they registered a pathway presence the way a dog registers a scent, without targeting. Two were predatory: at tier 3 and above, a practitioner's signature drew them if they were in the area. At tier 1, he would register to the passive types. The Guide said this was generally neutral. The passive entities avoided pathway practitioners rather than approaching them. One account noted a passive entity that had followed a tier 1 practitioner for three days across a region before losing interest.

He marked this as a variable to monitor after advancement. Not urgent, but real.

He looked at his accumulated total before turning the lamp down on the eighth night.

Confirmed: 20 units. Tentative: 5 additional from the cabin. Conservative threshold: 30 units. He needed either 10 confirmed units or 5 if the cabin sessions held.

Ten dark sessions at confirmed pace, or one additional domain event to accelerate.

He could not schedule the second option. He would take the dark sessions as baseline and treat anything additional as acceleration.

'Fourteen days at minimum, possibly fewer. The 30-day window has 22 days remaining. No urgency. Pace is viable.'

He turned the lamp down and started the hour.
